   - Clear the `TF0` flag (though hardware may do this automatically).  
   - Return from interrupt.

ORG 0000H
    LJMP MAIN               ; Jump to main program on reset

ORG 000BH                  ; Timer 0 Interrupt Vector
    LJMP T0_ISR            ; Jump to Timer 0 ISR on rollover

; Main Program Start
ORG 0030H

MAIN:
    ; Initialize Stack Pointer (optional but recommended)
    MOV SP, #30H           ; Set stack pointer to 30H

    ; Configure Timer 0 as Counter in Mode 2 (auto‑reload)
    MOV TMOD, #06H         ; TMOD = 0000 0110b:
                           ;   Timer 0: Counter (C/T=1), Mode 2 (M1=1, M0=0)
                           ;   Timer 1: ignored (set to 0)

    ; Set initial counter value and reload value
    MOV TH0, #00H          ; Reload value = 00H (rollover from FF to 00)
    MOV TL0, #00H          ; Start counting from 00H

    ; Initialize Ports (optional, starts from 0)
    MOV P1, #00H           ; Clear Port 1
    MOV P2, #00H           ; Clear Port 2

    ; Enable Timer 0 Interrupt and Global Interrupts
    SETB ET0               ; IE.1 = 1 (enable Timer 0 interrupt)
    SETB EA                ; IE.7 = 1 (enable all interrupts)

    ; Start Timer 0 Counter
    SETB TR0               ; TCON.4 = 1 (start Timer 0)

    ; Main Loop: Continuously update P2 with current count in TL0
LOOP:
    MOV P2, TL0            ; Display current count on Port 2
    SJMP LOOP              ; Repeat indefinitely

;*******************************************************************************
; Timer 0 Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)
; Executed automatically when TL0 overflows (rolls over from FF to reload value)
;*******************************************************************************
T0_ISR:
    INC P1                 ; Increment Port 1 on each rollover

    ; Clear Timer 0 Overflow Flag (optional, hardware may clear it on ISR entry)
    CLR TF0                ; TCON.5 = 0 (clear overflow flag)

    RETI                   ; Return from interrupt

END

#### Explanation of Critical Instructions

1. **`MOV TMOD, #06H`**: Configures Timer 0 as an 8‑bit counter with auto‑reload (mode 2).  
2. **`MOV TH0, #00H` and `MOV TL0, #00H`**: Initialize the counter and reload value to zero.  
3. **`SETB ET0` and `SETB EA`**: Enable the Timer 0 interrupt and global interrupts.  
4. **`SETB TR0`**: Starts the counter; it now increments on external pulses at pin P3.4 (T0).  
5. **`MOV P2, TL0`**: The main loop constantly writes the current count (TL0) to Port P2.  
6. **`INC P1`**: Inside the ISR, Port P1 is incremented each time the counter rolls over.  
7. **`CLR TF0`**: Explicitly clears the Timer 0 overflow flag. In some 8051 variants, this flag is cleared automatically upon entering the ISR, but it is safe to clear it again.

ORG 0000H
    LJMP MAIN

ORG 0030H
MAIN:
    MOV TMOD, #06H        ; Timer 0: counter, mode 2
    MOV TH0, #00H         ; Reload value
    MOV TL0, #00H         ; Start count
    MOV P1, #00H          ; Clear P1
    MOV P2, #00H          ; Clear P2
    SETB TR0              ; Start Timer 0

LOOP:
    JNB TF0, NO_OVERFLOW  ; Check overflow flag
    INC P1                ; Increment P1 on overflow
    CLR TF0               ; Clear flag manually
NO_OVERFLOW:
    MOV P2, TL0           ; Update P2 with current count
    SJMP LOOP             ; Repeat

END

#### Notes

- The crystal frequency (11.0592 MHz) is not used in calculations because the timer is configured as a counter, driven by external pulses rather than the internal clock.
- In mode 2, the rollover rate depends on the frequency of the external signal applied to T0, not on the crystal.
- The program assumes no other tasks are running; the main loop dedicates all processor time to updating P2. In a more complex system, the interrupt method is preferable as it allows the main loop to perform other tasks.
- The initial values of TH0 and TL0 can be changed to alter the count range and rollover point. Here, they are set to zero for a full 0–255 count cycle.

question.content: Assume a switch is connected to $P2.4$. Write an 8051 assembly program to monitor the status of the switch. If the switch is ON (logic $1$), then generate a square wave of $2\ \text{kHz}$ at $P2.1$, else generate a square wave of $5\ \text{kHz}$ at $P2.7$. Use $\text{XTAL} = 12\ \text{MHz}$.

answer.content:
assembly
;======================================================================
; 8051 Assembly Program: Switch-Controlled Square Wave Generator
;======================================================================
; Specifications:
;   - Switch connected to P2.4
;   - If switch ON (logic 1): generate 2 kHz square wave at P2.1
;   - If switch OFF (logic 0): generate 5 kHz square wave at P2.7
;   - Crystal frequency: 12 MHz → Timer clock = 1 MHz (1 µs per count)
;   - Half-period calculations:
;       2 kHz: T = 500 µs → half-period = 250 µs → Timer reload = 256-250 = 6
;       5 kHz: T = 200 µs → half-period = 100 µs → Timer reload = 256-100 = 156
;======================================================================

        ORG 0000H               ; Reset vector
        LJMP MAIN               ; Jump to main program

        ORG 000BH               ; Timer 0 interrupt vector
        LJMP T0_ISR             ; Jump to Timer 0 interrupt service routine

        ORG 0030H               ; Main program start address

;======================================================================
; MAIN PROGRAM: Initialization and switch monitoring loop
;======================================================================
MAIN:
        MOV SP, #60H            ; Initialize stack pointer

        ; Configure ports
        SETB P2.4               ; Set P2.4 as input (write 1 to latch)
        CLR P2.1                ; Ensure P2.1 starts low
        CLR P2.7                ; Ensure P2.7 starts low

        ; Configure Timer 0 for mode 2 (8‑bit auto‑reload)
        MOV TMOD, #02H          ; Timer 0, mode 2, timer mode (02H)

        ; Initialize based on switch status
        JB P2.4, SW_ON_INIT     ; If P2.4=1, switch is ON
        ; Switch OFF initialization
        CLR 00H                 ; MODE = 0 (5 kHz at P2.7)
        MOV TH0, #156           ; Reload value for 100 µs half‑period
        MOV TL0, #156           ; Set initial count
        SJMP START_TIMER

SW_ON_INIT:
        SETB 00H                ; MODE = 1 (2 kHz at P2.1)
        MOV TH0, #6             ; Reload value for 250 µs half‑period
        MOV TL0, #6             ; Set initial count

START_TIMER:
        SETB TR0                ; Start Timer 0
        SETB ET0                ; Enable Timer 0 interrupt
        SETB EA                 ; Enable global interrupts

;----------------------------------------------------------------------
; Switch monitoring loop
;----------------------------------------------------------------------
CHECK_LOOP:
        JB P2.4, SWITCH_ON      ; Branch if switch is ON (P2.4 = 1)

        ; Switch is OFF
        JNB 00H, NO_CHANGE      ; If MODE already 0, no change needed
        CLR 00H                 ; Set MODE = 0
        MOV TH0, #156           ; Update Timer 0 reload value for 5 kHz
        MOV TL0, #156           ; Reset timer count
        CLR P2.1                ; Ensure P2.1 is low (inactive)
        SJMP NO_CHANGE

SWITCH_ON:
        JB 00H, NO_CHANGE       ; If MODE already 1, no change needed
        SETB 00H                ; Set MODE = 1
        MOV TH0, #6             ; Update Timer 0 reload value for 2 kHz
        MOV TL0, #6             ; Reset timer count
        CLR P2.7                ; Ensure P2.7 is low (inactive)

NO_CHANGE:
        SJMP CHECK_LOOP         ; Repeat indefinitely

;======================================================================
; TIMER 0 INTERRUPT SERVICE ROUTINE
; Generates square wave on the appropriate pin based on MODE
;======================================================================
T0_ISR:
        PUSH PSW                ; Save processor status word
        PUSH ACC                ; Save accumulator
        JB 00H, MODE_2KHZ       ; Jump if MODE = 1 (2 kHz)

        ; MODE = 0: Generate 5 kHz square wave on P2.7
        CPL P2.7                ; Toggle P2.7
        CLR P2.1                ; Keep P2.1 low (inactive)
        SJMP ISR_DONE

MODE_2KHZ:
        ; MODE = 1: Generate 2 kHz square wave on P2.1
        CPL P2.1                ; Toggle P2.1
        CLR P2.7                ; Keep P2.7 low (inactive)

ISR_DONE:
        POP ACC                 ; Restore accumulator
        POP PSW                 ; Restore processor status word
        RETI                    ; Return from interrupt

;======================================================================
; END OF PROGRAM
;======================================================================
        END

question.content: Write an 8051 (AT89C51) ALP to generate a square wave with a frequency of 2 kHz using a timer. Assume a clock frequency of 12 MHz. Do not access the TL0 or TL1 registers in the ALP. Provide the necessary timer calculations.

**Timer Calculations:**
- Clock frequency = 12 MHz
- Timer clock frequency = Clock frequency / 12 = 1 MHz (since each timer tick takes 12 oscillator periods)
- Timer period = 1 / 1 MHz = 1 $\mu$s
- Required square wave frequency = 2 kHz
- Time period of square wave = 1 / 2 kHz = 500 $\mu$s
- Half period (for toggling) = 500 $\mu$s / 2 = 250 $\mu$s
- Number of timer ticks for half period = 250 $\mu$s / 1 $\mu$s = 250
- Timer count value = 65536 - 250 = 65286 (for Timer 0/1 in 16-bit mode)
- TH0 = 65286 / 256 = 255 (0xFF)
- TL0 = 65286 % 256 = 6 (0x06)
**Assembly Language Program:**

ORG 0000H
LJMP MAIN
ORG 000BH
LJMP TIMER0_ISR
MAIN:
MOV TMOD, #01H ; Timer 0, mode 1 (16-bit)
MOV TH0, #0FFH ; Load high byte
MOV TL0, #06H ; Load low byte
SETB TR0 ; Start Timer 0
SETB ET0 ; Enable Timer 0 interrupt
SETB EA ; Enable global interrupt
SJMP $ ; Infinite loop
TIMER0_ISR:
CPL P1.0 ; Toggle pin P1.0 to generate square wave
MOV TH0, #0FFH ; Reload high byte
MOV TL0, #06H ; Reload low byte
RETI
END

answer.content:
### **Solution: Generating a 2 kHz Square Wave Using Timer on 8051**

**Objective**: Generate a square wave of frequency 2 kHz using an 8051 timer, given a crystal frequency of 12 MHz, without accessing the TL0/TL1 registers directly.

---

### **1. Timer Mode Selection and Calculations**

Since direct access to TL0/TL1 is prohibited, we must use a timer mode that **automatically reloads** the initial count value. This is achieved with **Mode 2** (8-bit auto-reload mode), where:
- The timer counts from the initial value in TLx up to 0xFF, overflows, and then reloads TLx with the value stored in THx.
- Only THx needs to be initialized; TLx is loaded automatically on overflow.

**Given**:
- Crystal frequency, \( f_{\text{osc}} = 12 \text{ MHz} \)
- Desired square wave frequency, \( f_{\text{sq}} = 2 \text{ kHz} \)
f_{\text{osc}} = 12 \text{ MHz}
f_{\text{sq}} = 2 \text{ kHz}

**Step 1: Determine timer clock frequency**  
In the 8051, the timer increments at \( \frac{f_{\text{osc}}}{12} \).
f_{\text{timer}} = \frac{12 \text{ MHz}}{12} = 1 \text{ MHz}
Thus, the timer clock period is:
T_{\text{timer}} = \frac{1}{f_{\text{timer}}} = 1 \ \mu\text{s}

**Step 2: Determine required toggle frequency for square wave**  
A square wave has equal high and low periods. To generate it, we toggle an output pin on each timer overflow. Thus, the **toggle frequency** is twice the square wave frequency:
f_{\text{toggle}} = 2 \times f_{\text{sq}} = 4 \text{ kHz}

**Step 3: Calculate timer overflow period**  
The overflow period \( T_{\text{overflow}} \) is the reciprocal of the toggle frequency:
T_{\text{overflow}} = \frac{1}{f_{\text{toggle}}} = \frac{1}{4 \text{ kHz}} = 250 \ \mu\text{s}

**Step 4: Compute number of timer counts needed**  
Each timer count corresponds to \( 1 \ \mu\text{s} \). The number of counts \( N \) required for 250 µs is:
N = \frac{T_{\text{overflow}}}{T_{\text{timer}}} = \frac{250 \ \mu\text{s}}{1 \ \mu\text{s}} = 250

**Step 5: Determine reload value for THx**  
In Mode 2, the timer is 8-bit, so it counts from the initial value \( M \) to 255 (0xFF). The number of counts per overflow is \( 256 - M \). We set:
Solving for \( M \):

Thus, we set **THx = 6** (or 0x06 in hexadecimal).

**Note**: Since TLx is not accessed, its initial value is unknown. After the first overflow, TLx is automatically reloaded with THx (6), so all subsequent periods will be accurate. The first pulse may have a slight timing error, which is acceptable for continuous waveform generation.

---

### **2. Algorithm and Program Flow**

1. **Configure Timer 0 in Mode 2** (auto-reload) by setting TMOD register.
2. **Initialize TH0** with the calculated reload value (6).
3. **Enable Timer 0 interrupt** by setting ET0 and EA bits.
4. **Start Timer 0** by setting TR0 bit.
5. In the **Timer 0 Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)**, toggle an output pin (e.g., P1.0).
6. The main program waits indefinitely while the ISR handles toggling.

---

### **3. Assembly Language Program**

assembly
;=======================================================
; 8051 ALP to generate 2 kHz square wave using Timer 0
; Crystal frequency: 12 MHz
; Mode: Timer 0, Mode 2 (auto-reload)
; Output: P1.0
; Do not access TL0/TL1 registers
;=======================================================

; Interrupt vector for Timer 0
        ORG 0000H
        LJMP MAIN          ; Reset vector
        ORG 000BH
        LJMP TIMER0_ISR    ; Timer 0 interrupt vector

; Main program
MAIN:
        MOV TMOD, #02H     ; Set Timer 0 in Mode 2 (auto-reload)
        MOV TH0, #06H      ; Load TH0 with reload value for 250 µs overflow
        SETB ET0           ; Enable Timer 0 interrupt
        SETB EA            ; Enable global interrupts
        SETB TR0           ; Start Timer 0
        SJMP $             ; Infinite loop (wait for interrupts)

; Timer 0 Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)
TIMER0_ISR:
        CPL P1.0           ; Toggle P1.0 to generate square wave
        RETI               ; Return from interrupt

        END


---

### **4. Explanation of Code**

- **TMOD register**: Setting `TMOD = 0x02` configures Timer 0 in Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload). The lower 4 bits are for Timer 0: `GATE=0`, `C/T=0` (timer mode), `M1=1`, `M0=0` (Mode 2).
- **TH0 initialization**: `TH0 = 0x06` ensures that after each overflow, TL0 is reloaded with 6, giving 250 counts (256 - 6) per overflow.
- **Interrupt enabling**: ET0 enables Timer 0 interrupt; EA enables all interrupts.
- **Timer start**: TR0 bit starts Timer 0.
- **ISR**: Each time Timer 0 overflows, the interrupt is triggered, and the ISR toggles P1.0, producing a square wave.

---

### **5. Timing Verification**

- Timer overflow period = 250 µs (as calculated).
- Toggle frequency = \( \frac{1}{250 \ \mu\text{s}} = 4 \text{ kHz} \).
- Square wave frequency = \( \frac{4 \text{ kHz}}{2} = 2 \text{ kHz} \), matching the requirement.

**Note**: The first overflow occurs after (256 – initial TL0) counts, which is unpredictable. However, after the first overflow, TL0 is reloaded with 6, and all subsequent periods are exactly 250 µs, yielding a stable 2 kHz square wave after an initial transient.

---

### **6. Alternative Approach Without Interrupts (Polling Method)**

If interrupts are not desired, the following polling-based code can be used. However, it keeps the CPU busy and is less efficient.

assembly
; Polling method (no interrupts)
        ORG 0000H
MAIN:
        MOV TMOD, #02H     ; Timer 0, Mode 2
        MOV TH0, #06H      ; Reload value
        SETB TR0           ; Start timer
LOOP:
        JNB TF0, $         ; Wait until overflow flag sets
        CLR TF0            ; Clear overflow flag manually
        CPL P1.0           ; Toggle P1.0
        SJMP LOOP          ; Repeat
        END


**Drawback**: The CPU is stuck in the polling loop, unable to perform other tasks. The interrupt-based method is preferred for real applications.

---

### **7. Summary of Timer Calculations**

Parameter Value Formula
Crystal frequency 12 MHz Given
Timer clock frequency 1 MHz \( f_{\text{osc}} / 12 \)
Timer clock period 1 µs \( 1 / f_{\text{timer}} \)
Desired square wave frequency 2 kHz Given
Required toggle frequency 4 kHz \( 2 \times f_{\text{sq}} \)
Timer overflow period 250 µs \( 1 / f_{\text{toggle}} \)
Number of timer counts 250 \( T_{\text{overflow}} / T_{\text{timer}} \)
TH0 reload value (hex) 0x06 \( 256 - 250 = 6 \)
This solution satisfies the constraint of not accessing TL0/TL1 registers by utilizing Timer 0 in auto-reload mode (Mode 2), ensuring accurate square wave generation after the initial timer cycle.

question.content: Write an 8051 (AT89C51) Assembly Language Program (ALP) to implement the following logic using interrupts INT0 and INT1:

- Configure the system so that an external interrupt on INT0 (edge-triggered) turns ON an LED connected to pin P1.3.
- Configure the system so that an external interrupt on INT1 (edge-triggered) turns OFF the same LED at pin P1.3.
- The main program should initialize the system with the given register values: $TCON = 01H$ and $IE = 0FFH$, and then enter an infinite loop.
- Write the necessary Interrupt Service Routines (ISRs) for INT0 and INT1 to handle the LED control.

answer.content:
### Solution

The 8051 microcontroller's interrupt system allows rapid response to external events. This program implements edge‑triggered external interrupts **INT0** and **INT1** to control an LED connected to pin **P1.3**.

#### 1. Interrupt Configuration & Initialization  
The initialization sets up the interrupt control registers as specified:

- **TCON (Timer/Counter Control Register)**: Setting `TCON = 01H` configures **INT0** as edge‑triggered (`IT0 = 1`). However, to also make **INT1** edge‑triggered (`IT1 = 1`), we must set bit 2 of TCON after the initial assignment.  
- **IE (Interrupt Enable Register)**: Setting `IE = 0FFH` globally enables all interrupts (`EA = 1`) and specifically enables **INT0** (`EX0 = 1`) and **INT1** (`EX1 = 1`).  

The main program initializes these registers, sets the initial LED state (OFF), and enters an infinite loop.

#### 2. Assembly Program  
assembly
        ORG 0000H           ; Reset vector
        LJMP MAIN           ; Jump to main program

        ORG 0003H           ; Interrupt vector for INT0
        LJMP ISR_INT0       ; Jump to INT0 ISR

        ORG 0013H           ; Interrupt vector for INT1
        LJMP ISR_INT1       ; Jump to INT1 ISR

        ORG 0030H           ; Main program start

MAIN:
        MOV TCON, #01H      ; Set TCON=01H (IT0=1 for edge‑triggered INT0)
        SETB TCON.2         ; Set IT1=1 for edge‑triggered INT1
        MOV IE, #0FFH       ; Enable all interrupts (IE=FFH)
        SETB P1.3           ; Initially turn LED OFF (assuming active‑low)

LOOP:
        SJMP LOOP           ; Infinite loop (wait for interrupts)

; --- Interrupt Service Routine for INT0 ---
ISR_INT0:
        CLR P1.3            ; Turn LED ON (clear pin P1.3)
        RETI                ; Return from interrupt

; --- Interrupt Service Routine for INT1 ---
ISR_INT1:
        SETB P1.3           ; Turn LED OFF (set pin P1.3)
        RETI                ; Return from interrupt

        END                 ; End of program
#### 3. Program Explanation

Section Purpose
Vector Setup Redirects the processor to the appropriate ISR when an interrupt occurs.
MAIN Initializes TCON, IE, and the LED pin; then loops indefinitely.
ISR_INT0 Clears P1.3 (LED ON) when INT0 is triggered.
ISR_INT1 Sets P1.3 (LED OFF) when INT1 is triggered.

**Assumptions:**
- The LED is connected in **active‑low** configuration (i.e., `P1.3 = 0` turns ON the LED, `P1.3 = 1` turns it OFF). If the hardware uses an active‑high connection, swap `CLR`/`SETB` instructions in the ISRs.
- The 8051’s internal pull‑ups are enabled on Port 1, providing sufficient drive for the LED.
#### 4. Visual Representation of Interrupt Flow

**Key Points:**
- **INT0** (pin P3.2) and **INT1** (pin P3.3) are configured as edge‑triggered by setting bits **IT0** and **IT1** in TCON.
- The **IE** register enables both external interrupts and the global interrupt enable bit.
- The interrupt vectors at 0003H and 0013H contain jumps to the respective ISRs.
- Each ISR performs a single bit operation on P1.3 and returns via `RETI`.

This solution meets all given specifications: correct initialization, edge‑triggered interrupts, and dedicated ISRs for turning the LED ON and OFF.

question.content: For the provided 8051 microcontroller assembly program, identify what the program does and write comment lines for each section or instruction in the code.

answer.content:
**Solution**

The program listed below (a representative example of a typical 8051 microcontroller assembly code) functions as a **software-generated square wave generator** or **LED blinker** on pin `P1.0`. It toggles the state of that pin repeatedly, with a delay between each toggle to create a visible or periodic signal.
### Program Listing with Detailed Comments

assembly
; ----------------------------------------------------------------------
; Program: Square Wave Generator / LED Blinker on P1.0
; Description: Toggles pin P1.0 continuously with a defined delay,
;              creating a periodic square wave.
; Hardware: Pin P1.0 is typically connected to an LED (with current-limiting resistor).
; ----------------------------------------------------------------------

        ORG  0000H           ; Origin directive: start program at memory address 0000H (reset vector)
        LJMP MAIN            ; Jump over the interrupt vector table to the main program

        ORG  0030H           ; Set program origin to 0030H (after interrupt vectors)

MAIN:   MOV  P1, #00H        ; Initialize Port 1: clear all pins (set to logic 0)
                                ; This ensures a known starting state (LED off if active-low).

LOOP:   CPL  P1.0            ; Complement (toggle) bit 0 of Port 1 (P1.0 = NOT P1.0)
                                ; If LED is active-low, this turns it ON if OFF, and OFF if ON.

        ACALL DELAY          ; Subroutine call: wait for a fixed duration to control frequency.
        SJMP LOOP            ; Short jump back to LOOP, creating an infinite loop.
                                ; This continuous toggle generates a square wave.

; ----------------------------------------------------------------------
; Subroutine: DELAY
; Description: Creates a software delay using nested loops.
;              The total delay time is a function of the 8051's clock frequency
;              and the number of loop iterations.
; Registers used: R0, R1 (bank 0 by default)
; ----------------------------------------------------------------------
DELAY:  MOV  R0, #0FFH       ; Load outer loop counter R0 with 255 ($FF)
DELAY1: MOV  R1, #0FFH       ; Load inner loop counter R1 with 255 ($FF)
DELAY2: DJNZ R1, DELAY2      ; Decrement R1 and jump to DELAY2 if not zero.
                                ; Inner loop: 255 iterations × 2 machine cycles ≈ 510 machine cycles per inner loop.
        DJNZ R0, DELAY1      ; Decrement R0 and jump to DELAY1 if not zero.
                                ; Outer loop repeats inner loop 255 times.
        RET                  ; Return from subroutine to the instruction after ACALL.
                                ; Total delay ≈ 255 × 255 × 2 machine cycles + overhead machine cycles.

        END                  ; End of assembly program.

### Explanation of Key Instructions and Sections

1. **`ORG 0000H` & `LJMP MAIN`**:  
   The 8051 begins execution at address `0000H` after reset. The `LJMP MAIN` instruction redirects execution to the labeled `MAIN` routine, bypassing the interrupt vector area.

2. **`MOV P1, #00H`**:  
   Port 1 is an 8-bit I/O port. This instruction writes the immediate value `00H` (all zeros) to the port latch, setting all pins (P1.0–P1.7) to a low logic level.

3. **`CPL P1.0`**:  
   **C**om**P**lement **L**ogical: inverts the bit value of the specified bit-addressable location (here, pin 0 of Port 1). This is the core toggle operation.

4. **`ACALL DELAY`**:  
   **A**bsolute **CALL** pushes the return address (next instruction’s address) onto the stack and jumps to the subroutine labeled `DELAY`.

5. **`SJMP LOOP`**:  
   **S**hort **J**ump (relative jump) unconditionally branches back to the label `LOOP`, creating an infinite loop.

6. **Nested Delay Loop (`DELAY` subroutine)**:  
   - Uses two registers (`$R0$`, `$R1$`) as loop counters.
   - `DJNZ` (**D**ecrement and **J**ump if **N**ot **Z**ero) is the primary looping construct.  
     - Each `DJNZ` instruction takes 2 machine cycles to execute (24 clock cycles on a standard 8051 with 12 clock cycles per machine cycle).
   - The total delay in machine cycles is approximately:  
     \[
     \text{Machine Cycles} \approx 255 \times 255 \times 2 = 130050
     \]  
     plus overhead for the `MOV` and `RET` instructions. For a 12 MHz crystal (1 machine cycle = 1 µs), this yields a delay of roughly 130 ms.
### Visual Representation of the Program Flow
This diagram illustrates the control flow: initialization, infinite toggle loop, and the nested delay subroutine structure.

question.content:
Write an 8051 (AT89C51) ALP to generate two periodic square waveforms simultaneously on specified I/O pins as follows:

i) $0.1\text{ ms}$ ON and $0.1\text{ ms}$ OFF
ii) $0.2\text{ ms}$ ON and $0.2\text{ ms}$ OFF

using the microcontroller's timers. Do not access the TL0 or TL1 registers in the ALP. Provide necessary timer calculations for setting up the timers based on the machine cycle and timer modes covered in the syllabus.

answer.content:
### **Solution: Generating Dual Square Waves with 8051 Timers**

This solution details the assembly language program (ALP) and timer calculations for generating two independent 50% duty‑cycle square waves using the AT89C51 microcontroller’s timers. The waveforms are:

1. **Waveform 1:** 0.1 ms ON and 0.1 ms OFF → toggle every 0.1 ms.  
2. **Waveform 2:** 0.2 ms ON and 0.2 ms OFF → toggle every 0.2 ms.

We assume a **12 MHz crystal frequency**, giving a machine‑cycle time of:
\text{Machine Cycle Time} = \frac{12\ \text{clock cycles}}{12\ \text{MHz}} = 1\ \mu\text{s}.

Timers are configured in **Mode 2 (8‑bit auto‑reload)** to avoid manually reloading TLx registers in the ISR, adhering to the constraint of not accessing TL0/TL1.

---

### **Timer Calculations**

#### **1. Timer 0: 0.1 ms Interrupt Interval**
- Desired time = 100 µs.
- Number of machine cycles = \(100\ \mu\text{s} / 1\ \mu\text{s} = 100\) counts.
- In Mode 2, reload value is stored in TH0.
- Calculation:

#### **2. Timer 1: 0.2 ms Interrupt Interval**
- Desired time = 200 µs.
- Number of machine cycles = \(200\ \mu\text{s} / 1\ \mu\text{s} = 200\) counts.
- Reload value:

> **Note:** The first interrupt for each timer occurs after 256 µs because TLx registers are initialized to 0 on reset and count from 0 to 255 (256 counts) before the first overflow. After the first overflow, THx (9Cₕ for Timer 0 and 38ₕ for Timer 1) is reloaded into TLx, and subsequent interrupts occur at the desired intervals (100 µs and 200 µs). This initial delay is acceptable under the given constraints.

---

### **Assembly Language Program (ALP)**

assembly
; -------------------------------------------------------------------
; AT89C51 ALP: Dual Square Wave Generator using Timer 0 and Timer 1
; Waveform 1: P1.0 - 0.1 ms ON, 0.1 ms OFF (0.2 ms period)
; Waveform 2: P1.1 - 0.2 ms ON, 0.2 ms OFF (0.4 ms period)
; Crystal: 12 MHz → Machine Cycle = 1 µs
; Timers in Mode 2 (Auto-Reload), No access to TL0/TL1 registers
; -------------------------------------------------------------------

        ORG 0000H               ; Reset vector
        LJMP MAIN               ; Jump to main program

        ORG 000BH               ; Timer 0 interrupt vector
        LJMP TIMER0_ISR         ; Jump to Timer 0 ISR

        ORG 001BH               ; Timer 1 interrupt vector
        LJMP TIMER1_ISR         ; Jump to Timer 1 ISR

        ORG 0030H               ; Main program start
MAIN:   MOV TMOD, #22H          ; Set both Timer 0 and Timer 1 in Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload)
        MOV TH0, #9CH           ; Load Timer 0 reload value for 100 µs (0.1 ms)
        MOV TH1, #38H           ; Load Timer 1 reload value for 200 µs (0.2 ms)
        SETB TR0                ; Start Timer 0
        SETB TR1                ; Start Timer 1
        SETB ET0                ; Enable Timer 0 interrupt
        SETB ET1                ; Enable Timer 1 interrupt
        SETB EA                 ; Enable global interrupts
        CLR P1.0                ; Initialize P1.0 output low
        CLR P1.1                ; Initialize P1.1 output low
        SJMP $                  ; Infinite loop (wait for interrupts)

; -------------------------------------------------------------------
; Timer 0 Interrupt Service Routine (ISR) - Toggles P1.0 every 100 µs
; -------------------------------------------------------------------
TIMER0_ISR:
        CPL P1.0                ; Toggle P1.0 (waveform 1)
        RETI                    ; Return from interrupt

; -------------------------------------------------------------------
; Timer 1 Interrupt Service Routine (ISR) - Toggles P1.1 every 200 µs
; -------------------------------------------------------------------
TIMER1_ISR:
        CPL P1.1                ; Toggle P1.1 (waveform 2)
        RETI                    ; Return from interrupt

        END                     ; End of program


---

### **Program Explanation**

#### **Initialization (MAIN)**
- **TMOD = 0x22:**  
  Bits 0–1 (M1=1, M0=0) → Timer 0 in Mode 2.  
  Bits 4–5 (M1=1, M0=0) → Timer 1 in Mode 2.
- **TH0 = 0x9C, TH1 = 0x38:** Set reload values calculated above.
- **TR0/TR1:** Start both timers.
- **ET0/ET1:** Enable timer interrupts.
- **EA:** Global interrupt enable.
- **P1.0/P1.1:** Initialized low (optional; toggling starts in ISR).

#### **Interrupt Service Routines (ISRs)**
- Each ISR simply toggles the assigned pin. The auto‑reload feature ensures the timer automatically reloads THx into TLx on overflow, so no manual reload is needed.

#### **Waveform Generation**
- **P1.0:** Toggles every 100 µs → 0.1 ms high, 0.1 ms low (0.2 ms period, 5 kHz).
- **P1.1:** Toggles every 200 µs → 0.2 ms high, 0.2 ms low (0.4 ms period, 2.5 kHz).

---

### **System Architecture Diagram**

> **AT89C51 Timer‑Based Waveform Generation**

**Description:**
- **Clock Source:** 12 MHz crystal provides the system clock.
- **Timers:** Timer 0 and Timer 1 operate independently in Mode 2 (auto‑reload).
- **Interrupt Flow:** On overflow, each timer triggers its dedicated ISR (vector addresses 000Bₕ and 001Bₕ).
- **ISR Actions:** Each ISR toggles a specific I/O pin (P1.0 or P1.1).
- **Output:** P1.0 and P1.1 produce the required square waveforms simultaneously.

---

### **Key Points**
- **Mode 2 (Auto‑Reload):** Eliminates the need to manually reload TLx in the ISR, satisfying the constraint.
- **Simultaneous Operation:** Both timers run independently, generating waveforms concurrently.
- **Initial Timing Offset:** The first interrupt for each timer occurs after 256 µs due to TLx resetting to 0, but subsequent intervals are correct.
- **Scalability:** For different frequencies, adjust THx values using the same calculation method.

This solution meets the problem requirements using standard 8051 timer features while adhering to the constraint of not accessing TL0/TL1 registers in the ALP.

question.content: For the Intel 8051 microcontroller, using assembly language, write a program that interfaces a switch connected to $P2.0$, an LED connected to $P1.1$, and a buzzer connected to $P2.2$. The program should continuously read the switch input and perform: if the input is $0$, turn ON the buzzer for $15\text{ms}$ using timers; if the input is $1$, turn ON the LED for $20\text{ms}$ using timers. Assume standard port configurations and timer settings as per the 8051 architecture.

Input (Switch at $P2.0$) Action Duration Output Device
$0$ Turn ON buzzer $15\text{ms}$ $P2.2$ (buzzer)
$1$ Turn ON LED $20\text{ms}$ $P1.1$ (LED)

answer.content:
### Solution: Interfacing Switch, LED, and Buzzer with Intel 8051 Assembly

This program for the Intel 8051 microcontroller continuously monitors a switch connected to **P2.0**. Based on the switch state, it activates either a **buzzer** (on **P2.2**) for **15 ms** or an **LED** (on **P1.1**) for **20 ms**, using Timer 0 for precise delays. The following assumptions are made:

- **Clock frequency**: 12 MHz (standard), yielding a timer increment period of 1 µs (since machine cycle = 12 / 12 MHz = 1 µs).
- **Port configurations**:
  - P2.0 is configured as an input (switch).
  - P1.1 and P2.2 are configured as outputs (LED and buzzer, respectively).
- **Active-high outputs**: A logic high (1) turns on the LED/buzzer.
- **Timer settings**: Timer 0 in Mode 1 (16-bit timer) is used for delay generation.

---

#### Timer Calculations

For a 12 MHz clock, each timer count corresponds to **1 µs**. The required counts for the delays are:

- **15 ms delay**:  
  Counts = 15,000  
  Initial value = 65,536 – 15,000 = 50,536 = 0xC568  
  ∴ TH0 = 0xC5, TL0 = 0x68

- **20 ms delay**:  
  Counts = 20,000  
  Initial value = 65,536 – 20,000 = 45,536 = 0xB1E0  
  ∴ TH0 = 0xB1, TL0 = 0xE0

---

#### Assembly Program

assembly
; ============================================
; Program: Switch, LED, and Buzzer Interface
; Microcontroller: Intel 8051
; Clock: 12 MHz
; ============================================

; --- Register and Port Definitions ---
SWITCH  BIT  P2.0      ; Switch connected to P2.0
LED     BIT  P1.1      ; LED connected to P1.1
BUZZER  BIT  P2.2      ; Buzzer connected to P2.2

; --- Program Start ---
        ORG  0000H     ; Reset vector
        LJMP MAIN      ; Jump to main program

        ORG  0030H     ; Main program start address

; --- Initialization ---
MAIN:
        MOV  TMOD, #01H    ; Timer 0, Mode 1 (16-bit timer)
        SETB SWITCH        ; Configure P2.0 as input (set for reading)
        CLR  LED           ; Turn off LED initially
        CLR  BUZZER        ; Turn off buzzer initially

; --- Main Loop ---
LOOP:
        MOV  C, SWITCH     ; Read switch state into carry flag
        JC   LED_ON        ; If carry = 1 (switch open), jump to LED_ON
        ; Otherwise, switch closed (input = 0): activate buzzer

BUZZER_ON:
        SETB BUZZER        ; Turn on buzzer
        LCALL DELAY_15MS   ; Call 15 ms delay
        CLR  BUZZER        ; Turn off buzzer
        SJMP LOOP          ; Repeat loop

LED_ON:
        SETB LED           ; Turn on LED
        LCALL DELAY_20MS   ; Call 20 ms delay
        CLR  LED           ; Turn off LED
        SJMP LOOP          ; Repeat loop

; --- Delay Subroutines ---

; Delay: 15 ms using Timer 0
DELAY_15MS:
        MOV  TH0, #0C5H    ; Load high byte for 15 ms
        MOV  TL0, #68H     ; Load low byte for 15 ms
        SETB TR0           ; Start Timer 0
WAIT_15:
        JNB  TF0, WAIT_15  ; Wait until Timer 0 overflows
        CLR  TR0           ; Stop Timer 0
        CLR  TF0           ; Clear overflow flag
        RET

; Delay: 20 ms using Timer 0
DELAY_20MS:
        MOV  TH0, #0B1H    ; Load high byte for 20 ms
        MOV  TL0, #0E0H    ; Load low byte for 20 ms
        SETB TR0           ; Start Timer 0
WAIT_20:
        JNB  TF0, WAIT_20  ; Wait until Timer 0 overflows
        CLR  TR0           ; Stop Timer 0
        CLR  TF0           ; Clear overflow flag
        RET

; --- End of Program ---
        END

---

#### Explanation of Key Sections

1. **Initialization**:
   - **TMOD = 0x01**: Configures Timer 0 in Mode 1 (16-bit timer).
   - **SETB SWITCH**: Configures P2.0 as an input by writing a logic high, enabling reading.
   - **CLR LED / BUZZER**: Ensures outputs are initially off.

2. **Main Loop**:
   - The switch state is read into the carry flag via `MOV C, SWITCH`.
   - If the carry flag is set (switch input = 1), the program jumps to `LED_ON`.
   - Otherwise (switch input = 0), it proceeds to `BUZZER_ON`.

3. **Output Activation**:
   - **Buzzer path**: Sets P2.2 high, calls `DELAY_15MS`, then clears P2.2.
   - **LED path**: Sets P1.1 high, calls `DELAY_20MS`, then clears P1.1.

4. **Delay Subroutines**:
   - Each subroutine loads Timer 0 with the pre-calculated initial value for the required delay.
   - Timer 0 is started (`SETB TR0`), and the program waits until the overflow flag (`TF0`) is set.
   - The timer is then stopped, the overflow flag cleared, and control returns.

**Connections**:
- **Switch**: Connected between P2.0 and ground (active-low; pressing switch gives logic 0).
- **LED**: Anode connected to P1.1 via a current-limiting resistor (330Ω) to +5V.
- **Buzzer**: Connected between P2.2 and ground (active-high).
- **Power**: VCC = +5V, GND = 0V.

---

#### Notes on Timer Accuracy

- The calculated delays assume a **12 MHz crystal** with no external capacitors affecting timing.
- The actual delay may vary slightly due to **instruction execution time** (the overhead of calling the subroutine and managing the timer). For higher precision, these overhead cycles can be compensated by adjusting the initial timer values.
- If a different clock frequency is used, the timer counts must be recalculated accordingly using:
\text{Counts} = \frac{\text{Delay (s)} \times \text{Crystal Frequency (Hz)}}{12}

---

#### Testing and Debugging Tips

1. **Software Simulation**: Use an 8051 simulator (e.g., µVision, Simplicity) to verify timing and port behavior.
2. **Hardware Verification**: 
   - Use an oscilloscope or logic analyzer to measure the pulse widths on P1.1 and P2.2.
   - Ensure the switch is debounced if needed (this code does not include debouncing).
3. **Modifications**:
   - For an **active-low LED/buzzer**, invert the set/clear instructions (e.g., `CLR LED` to turn on).
   - To avoid blocking during delays (e.g., for concurrent tasks), use timer interrupts, but this is beyond the scope of the problem.

This solution fulfills the requirements by integrating switch reading, conditional output activation, and timer-based delays in a structured assembly program.

Input/Output Logic
Switch (P2.0) Active-low: 0 when pressed, 1 when open
LED (P1.1) Active-high: 1 turns on, 0 turns off
Buzzer (P2.2) Active-high: 1 turns on, 0 turns off

question.content: The 8051 microcontroller is connected to a water level sensor through ADC0808, which gives 5V when the water tank is 100% full. Develop an assembly program with a suitable interfacing diagram to interface ADC0808 with 8051 to compare the water level stored in accumulator ($A$) with the threshold value $E6_{H}$. If $A > E6_{H}$, send a message 'HIGH LEVEL' through the serial port at a baud rate of 2400. Assume 'HIGH LEVEL' is stored in ROM location $500_{H}$ onwards.

answer.content:
### Interfacing Diagram (Text Description)

* **8051 Microcontroller** (left side):
  - **Port 2 (P2.0–P2.7)**: Connected to ADC0808 data outputs D0–D7 (8‑bit data bus).
  - **Port 1.0**: Output pin connected to ADC0808 SC (Start Conversion) and ALE (Address Latch Enable) tied together.
  - **Port 1.1**: Output pin connected to ADC0808 OE (Output Enable).
  - **Port 1.2**: Input pin connected to ADC0808 EOC (End of Conversion).
  - **Port 3.1 (TXD)**: Serial output for transmitting message.
  - **XTAL1, XTAL2**: Connected to 11.0592 MHz crystal oscillator.
  - **RESET**: Circuit with capacitor and resistor to Vcc and ground.
  - **EA (–)**: Tied to Vcc for internal program memory.
  - **Vcc (+5 V)** and **GND**: Power connections.

* **ADC0808** (right side):
  - **D0–D7**: Data outputs connected to 8051 Port 2.
  - **SC & ALE**: Combined input connected to 8051 P1.0.
  - **OE**: Input connected to 8051 P1.1.
  - **EOC**: Output connected to 8051 P1.2.
  - **CLK**: External clock input (typical 500 kHz, can be derived from 8051 ALE via divider if needed).
  - **Vref+**: Connected to +5 V.
  - **Vref-**: Connected to GND.
  - **IN0**: Analog input from water level sensor (0–5 V).
  - **A, B, C (address lines)**: All tied to GND to select channel 0.
  - **Vcc (+5 V)** and **GND**: Power connections.

* **Water Level Sensor**: Output (0–5 V) connected to ADC0808 IN0.

* **Serial Output**: 8051 TXD (P3.1) may be connected to a level converter (e.g., MAX232) for RS‑232 communication to a PC or display (not detailed here).

* **Pull‑up Resistors**: Port 2 has internal pull‑ups; if Port 0 were used, external 10 kΩ pull‑ups would be required on each line.

---

### Assembly Program

The program continuously reads the water level from the ADC, compares it with the threshold `E6H` (230), and transmits the string `"HIGH LEVEL"` via the serial port at 2400 baud if the level exceeds the threshold.
\text{THRESH} = \text{E6H} = 230 \quad (\approx 90\% \text{ of full scale})

assembly
;------------------------------------------------------------------
; 8051 Assembly Program: Water Level Monitoring with ADC0808
; Interface: ADC data on Port 2, control on Port 1 bits.
; Serial output: 2400 baud, crystal 11.0592 MHz.
; String "HIGH LEVEL" stored at ROM address 500H (null-terminated).
;------------------------------------------------------------------

; Symbolic definitions for ADC control signals
SC_ALE   BIT   P1.0      ; Start Conversion & Address Latch Enable
OE       BIT   P1.1      ; Output Enable (active high)
EOC      BIT   P1.2      ; End of Conversion (input)

; Threshold value
THRESH   EQU   0E6H      ; Threshold = 230 (≈90% of full scale)

;------------------------------------------------------------------
; Main program
;------------------------------------------------------------------
        ORG   0000H      ; Reset vector
        LJMP  MAIN

        ORG   0030H      ; Main program start
MAIN:
        ACALL INIT_SERIAL   ; Initialize serial port for 2400 baud
READ_ADC:
        ACALL GET_ADC_VALUE ; Read ADC value into accumulator A
        ; Compare A with threshold THRESH (E6H)
        CLR   C            ; Clear carry for subtraction
        SUBB  A, #THRESH   ; A = A - THRESH
        JZ    NO_MSG       ; If A = THRESH, no message
        JNC   SEND_STRING  ; If carry=0 (A > THRESH), send message
        ; If carry=1 (A < THRESH), fall through to NO_MSG
NO_MSG:
        SJMP  READ_ADC     ; Repeat monitoring

SEND_STRING:
        MOV   DPTR, #500H  ; Point DPTR to string in ROM
NEXT_CHAR:
        CLR   A            ; Clear accumulator for ROM read
        MOVC  A, @A+DPTR   ; Read character from ROM
        JZ    READ_ADC     ; If null terminator, go back to monitoring
        ACALL SEND_CHAR    ; Otherwise, transmit character
        INC   DPTR         ; Point to next character
        SJMP  NEXT_CHAR    ; Loop until null

;------------------------------------------------------------------
; Subroutine: Initialize serial port for 2400 baud
; Assumes 11.0592 MHz crystal, SMOD=0, Timer 1 mode 2.
;------------------------------------------------------------------
INIT_SERIAL:
        MOV   TMOD, #20H   ; Timer 1, mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload)
        MOV   TH1, #0F4H   ; Reload value for 2400 baud (SMOD=0)
        MOV   TL1, #0F4H   ; Initial value
        SETB  TR1          ; Start Timer 1
        MOV   SCON, #50H   ; Serial mode 1, enable receiver (REN=1)
        RET

;------------------------------------------------------------------
; Subroutine: Read ADC0808 value into accumulator A
; Steps: 1. Pulse SC/ALE to start conversion.
;        2. Wait for EOC to go high (conversion complete).
;        3. Set OE high, read data from Port 2, set OE low.
;------------------------------------------------------------------
GET_ADC_VALUE:
        CLR   OE           ; Ensure OE is low initially
        SETB  SC_ALE       ; Start conversion: raise SC/ALE
        CLR   SC_ALE       ; Lower SC/ALE (positive pulse)
WAIT_EOC:
        JNB   EOC, WAIT_EOC ; Wait while EOC is low (conversion in progress)
        ; When EOC goes high, conversion is complete
        SETB  OE           ; Enable ADC outputs
        MOV   A, P2        ; Read data from Port 2 into A
        CLR   OE           ; Disable ADC outputs
        RET

;------------------------------------------------------------------
; Subroutine: Transmit character in accumulator A via serial port
; Waits for TI flag, clears it after transmission.
;------------------------------------------------------------------
SEND_CHAR:
        MOV   SBUF, A      ; Load character into serial buffer
WAIT_TI:
        JNB   TI, WAIT_TI  ; Wait until transmission complete (TI=1)
        CLR   TI           ; Clear transmit flag for next character
        RET

;------------------------------------------------------------------
; Data: String "HIGH LEVEL" stored at 500H (null-terminated)
;------------------------------------------------------------------
        ORG   500H
        DB    'HIGH LEVEL', 0   ; ASCII string with null terminator

        END

**Explanation of Key Steps:**

1. **Serial Initialization**: Timer 1 is configured in mode 2 with reload value `F4H` to generate 2400 baud with an 11.0592 MHz crystal (SMOD = 0). The serial port is set to mode 1 (8‑bit UART).

2. **ADC Reading**:  
   - A low‑to‑high‑to‑low pulse on `SC_ALE` starts the conversion.  
   - The program polls the `EOC` line until it goes high, indicating conversion completion.  
   - `OE` is then set high to enable the ADC’s output buffers, and the digital value is read from Port 2.

3. **Threshold Comparison**: The value in $A$ is compared with `E6H` using a subtraction (`SUBB`). If the result is zero, the value is equal; if the carry flag is cleared (and the result is non‑zero), the value is greater. Only in the latter case is the message transmitted.

4. **String Transmission**: The data pointer (`DPTR`) is set to 500H, and characters are fetched from ROM using `MOVC` until a null byte is encountered. Each character is sent via the serial port using the `SEND_CHAR` subroutine, which waits for the `TI` flag to indicate completion.

5. **Continuous Operation**: After sending the message (or if the threshold is not exceeded), the program loops back to read the ADC again, providing continuous monitoring.

This solution fulfills the requirement of interfacing the ADC0808 with the 8051, comparing the water level with a threshold, and transmitting an alert message when necessary.

question.content: Write an 8051 assembly program to interface a 16x2 LCD display with a suitable diagram. Assume the string "HELLO" is stored in ROM location starting from $300H$ onwards. A switch is connected to port pin $P2.7$. If the switch input is $1$, display the string in line 1; otherwise, display the string in reverse order in line 2 as shown below.

Switch $P2.7$ Display
$1$ HELLO
$0$ OLLEH

answer.content:
### Solution: Interfacing a 16×2 LCD with 8051 Microcontroller

This solution provides an 8051 assembly program to interface a 16×2 LCD display. The program reads a switch connected to $P2.7$: if the switch is high (1), it displays the string "HELLO" on line 1; if low (0), it displays the reversed string "OLLEH" on line 2. The string is stored in ROM starting at address $300H$.

#### 1. Hardware Interfacing Diagram

Since an image cannot be generated, a detailed textual description of the connections is provided below.

> **Visual Representation: 8051 to 16×2 LCD Interface**
> 
> **Components**:
> - 8051 Microcontroller (e.g., AT89C51)
> - 16×2 LCD Module (e.g., HD44780-based)
> - Single-pole single-throw (SPST) switch connected to $P2.7$ with a pull-down resistor (10 $k\Omega$ to ground)
> 
> **Pin Connections**:
> 
> *Data Bus (8-bit mode)*:
> - LCD $D0$–$D7$ → 8051 Port 1 ($P1.0$–$P1.7$)
> 
> *Control Lines*:
> - LCD RS (Register Select) → 8051 $P3.0$
>   - $RS = 0$ for command, $RS = 1$ for data
> - LCD RW (Read/Write) → 8051 $P3.1$
>   - $RW = 0$ for write, $RW = 1$ for read
> - LCD EN (Enable) → 8051 $P3.2$
>   - High-to-low pulse to latch data/command
> 
> *Switch Interface*:
> - One terminal of switch → +5V (VCC)
> - Other terminal → 8051 $P2.7$ and also to pull-down resistor (10 $k\Omega$ to ground)
> 
> *Power*:
> - VCC and backlight LED anode → +5V
> - VSS, backlight LED cathode (if no internal resistor) → Ground
> - VEE (contrast) → Potentiometer wiper (e.g., 10 $k\Omega$ pot between VCC and ground)
> 
> **Layout Sketch**:
> 
>        +5V
>         |
>         |
>      SWITCH
>         |
>         +--- P2.7 (8051)
>         |
>     10kΩ (to GND)
> 
>  8051 Port 1 (P1.0–P1.7)  ===  LCD D0–D7
>  8051 P3.0 (RS)           ---> LCD RS
>  8051 P3.1 (RW)           ---> LCD RW
>  8051 P3.2 (EN)           ---> LCD EN
> 
> The LCD is placed to the right of the 8051, with data lines horizontally connected and control lines vertically aligned. The switch is placed above the 8051, connected to $P2.7$.

#### 2. Assembly Program

The program is structured into initialization, switch checking, and display routines. Key subroutines handle LCD commands, data writing, and delays. The string "HELLO" is stored in code memory at $300H$.

assembly
;====================================================================
; 8051 LCD Interface Program
; Description: Displays "HELLO" on line 1 if P2.7=1, else displays
;              "OLLEH" on line 2 if P2.7=0.
; String stored at ROM address 300H.
; Assumptions: 8-bit LCD data mode, control pins as defined below.
;====================================================================

; Control Pin Definitions (using bit-addressable pins)
RS      BIT     P3.0        ; Register Select
RW      BIT     P3.1        ; Read/Write
EN      BIT     P3.2        ; Enable

; Switch Pin
SWITCH  BIT     P2.7        ; Switch input

; LCD Commands
LCD_CLR EQU     01H         ; Clear display
LCD_HOME EQU    02H         ; Return home
LCD_LN1  EQU    80H         ; DDRAM address for line 1 start
LCD_LN2  EQU    0C0H        ; DDRAM address for line 2 start
LCD_ON   EQU    0EH         ; Display on, cursor on
LCD_8BIT EQU    38H         ; 8-bit, 2 lines, 5x7 font

; Program and Data Memory Organization
        ORG     0000H       ; Reset vector
        LJMP    MAIN        ; Jump to main program

        ORG     0300H       ; String storage (as per question)
STRING: DB      'H','E','L','L','O'

        ORG     0050H       ; Main program start (after interrupt vectors)

;====================================================================
; MAIN PROGRAM
;====================================================================
MAIN:
        MOV     SP, #70H    ; Initialize stack pointer
        ACALL   LCD_INIT    ; Initialize LCD
        ACALL   CHECK_SW    ; Check switch and display accordingly
        SJMP    $           ; Infinite loop (halt)

;====================================================================
; SUBROUTINE: CHECK_SW
; Description: Checks switch at P2.7 and calls appropriate display.
;====================================================================
CHECK_SW:
        JB      SWITCH, DISP_FWD   ; If switch=1, display forward
        ; else display reverse
        MOV     A, #LCD_LN2        ; Set cursor to line 2
        ACALL   LCD_CMD
        ACALL   DISP_REV
        RET
DISP_FWD:
        MOV     A, #LCD_LN1        ; Set cursor to line 1
        ACALL   LCD_CMD
        ACALL   DISP_FWD_STR
        RET

;====================================================================
; SUBROUTINE: DISP_FWD_STR
; Description: Displays string from 300H in forward order (HELLO).
; Uses DPTR as pointer, loops 5 times (string length).
;====================================================================
DISP_FWD_STR:
        MOV     DPTR, #STRING      ; Point to string start
        MOV     R2, #05H           ; Counter = 5 characters
FWD_LOOP:
        CLR     A                  ; Clear A for MOVC
        MOVC    A, @A+DPTR         ; Read character from code memory
        ACALL   LCD_DATA           ; Send to LCD
        INC     DPTR               ; Point to next character
        DJNZ    R2, FWD_LOOP       ; Decrement counter, loop if not zero
        RET

;====================================================================
; SUBROUTINE: DISP_REV
; Description: Displays string from 300H in reverse order (OLLEH).
; DPTR starts at last character (STRING+4) and decrements.
;====================================================================
DISP_REV:
        MOV     DPTR, #STRING+4    ; Point to last character ('O')
        MOV     R2, #05H           ; Counter = 5 characters
REV_LOOP:
        CLR     A                  ; Clear A for MOVC
        MOVC    A, @A+DPTR         ; Read character from code memory
        ACALL   LCD_DATA           ; Send to LCD
        DEC     DPTR               ; Point to previous character
        DJNZ    R2, REV_LOOP       ; Decrement counter, loop if not zero
        RET

;====================================================================
; SUBROUTINE: LCD_INIT
; Description: Initializes LCD with 8-bit mode, 2 lines, 5x7 font,
; display on, clear display, and entry mode.
;====================================================================
LCD_INIT:
        ACALL   DELAY              ; Power-on delay
        MOV     A, #LCD_8BIT       ; Function set: 8-bit, 2 lines, 5x7
        ACALL   LCD_CMD
        MOV     A, #LCD_ON         ; Display on, cursor on
        ACALL   LCD_CMD
        MOV     A, #LCD_CLR        ; Clear display
        ACALL   LCD_CMD
        MOV     A, #06H            ; Entry mode: increment, no shift
        ACALL   LCD_CMD
        RET

;====================================================================
; SUBROUTINE: LCD_CMD
; Description: Sends command in accumulator to LCD.
; Steps: RS=0 (command), RW=0 (write), send data, pulse EN high-low.
;====================================================================
LCD_CMD:
        CLR     RS                 ; RS=0 for command
        SJMP    LCD_WRITE          ; Jump to common write routine

;====================================================================
; SUBROUTINE: LCD_DATA
; Description: Sends data in accumulator to LCD.
; Steps: RS=1 (data), RW=0 (write), send data, pulse EN high-low.
;====================================================================
LCD_DATA:
        SETB    RS                 ; RS=1 for data
        ; Fall through to LCD_WRITE

LCD_WRITE:
        CLR     RW                 ; RW=0 for write
        MOV     P1, A              ; Put data/command on Port 1
        SETB    EN                 ; EN=1
        ACALL   DELAY_SHORT        ; Pulse width > 450 ns
        CLR     EN                 ; EN=0 (high-to-low pulse latches data)
        ACALL   DELAY              ; Wait for LCD to process
        RET

;====================================================================
; DELAY SUBROUTINES
; Note: Actual delay values depend on crystal frequency.
; Assume 12 MHz clock (1 µs per machine cycle).
;====================================================================
DELAY:
        MOV     R3, #50            ; Outer loop count
DELAY_LOOP:
        MOV     R4, #255           ; Inner loop count
        DJNZ    R4, $              ; 2 cycles per iteration
        DJNZ    R3, DELAY_LOOP
        RET

DELAY_SHORT:                       ; Very short delay for EN pulse
        MOV     R5, #10
        DJNZ    R5, $
        RET

;====================================================================
        END

#### 3. Program Explanation

- **Initialization**: The LCD is initialized by sending a series of commands: function set (8‑bit, 2 lines), display on, clear display, and entry mode set.
- **Switch Checking**: The program reads $P2.7$. If it is high (`JB SWITCH, DISP_FWD`), it sets the cursor to line 1 (address $80H$) and calls `DISP_FWD_STR`. If low, it sets the cursor to line 2 (address $C0H$) and calls `DISP_REV`.
- **Forward Display**: `DISP_FWD_STR` uses DPTR to point to the string start ($300H$) and reads each character sequentially using `MOVC A, @A+DPTR`. It sends five characters to the LCD.
- **Reverse Display**: `DISP_REV` initializes DPTR to $304H$ (last character) and decrements DPTR after each character, sending the string in reverse order.
- **LCD Communication**: The subroutines `LCD_CMD` and `LCD_DATA` handle the control signals ($RS$, $RW$, $EN$) and data transfer via Port 1.
- **Delays**: `DELAY` provides sufficient wait time for LCD operations; `DELAY_SHORT` ensures the $EN$ pulse width meets HD44780 timing requirements.
(50 \times 255 \times 2) + \text{overhead} \approx 25,500\ \mu\text{s} = 25.5\ \text{ms}

This meets the LCD power‑on and command execution delays (typically >15 ms). The `DELAY_SHORT` provides about 20 $\mu$s, sufficient for the $EN$ pulse width (>450 ns). Adjust delays if a different crystal is used.

#### 5. Alternative Approach (4‑bit Mode)

For reduced I/O usage, the LCD can be interfaced in 4‑bit mode (using only $P1.4$–$P1.7$). This would require modifying the initialization and data/command routines to send two nibbles per byte. However, the given problem does not specify the data mode, so the standard 8‑bit mode is used for simplicity.

Switch State ($P2.7$) LCD Line Displayed String
High (1) Line 1 HELLO
Low (0) Line 2 OLLEH

Module 5: I/O interfacing with Microcont.visual_description: Markdown table describing LCD output based on switch state.

question.content: Develop an 8051 ALP to interface a capacitive touch sensor with Port $P3.4$ and use Timer $0$ to detect touch events. When the sensor is touched, increment the count on Port $P1$ and display the touch count on Port $P2$ every $1$ second using Timer $1$.

answer.content:
### **Solution: 8051 Assembly Language Program for Capacitive Touch Sensor Interface**

#### **Objective**
Develop an assembly language program (ALP) for the 8051 microcontroller to:
1. Interface a capacitive touch sensor with pin **P3.4** (T0 input).
2. Use **Timer 0** as a counter to detect touch events.
3. On each touch, increment a count and display it immediately on **Port P1**.
4. Use **Timer 1** to generate a 1-second periodic update, displaying the same count on **Port P2** every second.

#### **Assumptions**
- Crystal frequency: **12 MHz**, giving a timer increment period of **1 $\mu$s**.
- Capacitive touch sensor produces a clean pulse on P3.4 for each touch.
- Timer 0 is configured in **mode 2** (8-bit auto-reload counter) to count pulses.
- Timer 1 is configured in **mode 1** (16-bit timer) to generate a **50 ms** interrupt; 20 such interrupts yield 1 second.
- `touch_count` is an 8-bit variable (range 0-255) stored at RAM location `30H`.
- Ports P1 and P2 are configured as output ports.

---

#### **Algorithm**
1. **Initialization**
   - Set stack pointer (`SP`) to a safe location.
   - Clear `touch_count` and initialize a counter for 1-second timing.
   - Configure **Timer 0** as a counter (mode 2) with reload value `0xFF` to overflow on each pulse.
   - Configure **Timer 1** as a timer (mode 1) with reload values for a 50 ms overflow.
   - Enable Timer 0 and Timer 1 interrupts, then start both timers.

2. **Interrupt Service Routines**
   - **Timer 0 ISR** (address `000BH`):
     - Increment `touch_count`.
     - Output updated `touch_count` to Port P1.
   - **Timer 1 ISR** (address `001BH`):
     - Reload Timer 1 for the next 50 ms.
     - Decrement the 1-second counter; when it reaches zero:
       - Output `touch_count` to Port P2.
       - Reset the counter to 20.

3. **Main Loop**
   - Enter an infinite idle loop, waiting for interrupts.

---

#### **Assembly Code**

assembly
;------------------------------------------------------------------
; 8051 ALP: Capacitive Touch Sensor Interface
; Sensor connected to P3.4, Timer0 as counter, Timer1 for 1-sec updates
; Port P1 shows real-time count; Port P2 updates every second
; Crystal: 12 MHz -> timer increment = 1 µs
;------------------------------------------------------------------

;------------------------------------------------------------------
; Register and RAM definitions
;------------------------------------------------------------------
TOUCH_COUNT   EQU   30H        ; RAM location for touch count (8-bit)
OVERFLOW_CNT  EQU   31H        ; Counter for 1-sec timing (20 × 50 ms)

;------------------------------------------------------------------
; Reset and interrupt vectors
;------------------------------------------------------------------
        ORG     0000H          ; Reset vector
        LJMP    MAIN           ; Jump to main program

        ORG     000BH          ; Timer 0 interrupt vector
        LJMP    TIMER0_ISR     ; Jump to Timer 0 ISR

        ORG     001BH          ; Timer 1 interrupt vector
        LJMP    TIMER1_ISR     ; Jump to Timer 1 ISR

        ORG     0030H          ; Main program start address

;------------------------------------------------------------------
; MAIN: Initialization and infinite loop
;------------------------------------------------------------------
MAIN:
        ; Initialize stack pointer
        MOV     SP, #60H       ; Set SP to 60H

        ; Initialize variables
        MOV     TOUCH_COUNT, #00H   ; Clear touch count
        MOV     OVERFLOW_CNT, #14H  ; 20 decimal = 14H (for 1 sec)

        ; Initialize Ports (optional, clear outputs)
        MOV     P1, #00H
        MOV     P2, #00H

        ; Configure Timer 0 and Timer 1 modes
        ; TMOD: T1 mode 1 (16-bit timer), T0 mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload counter)
        ; T1: Gate=0, C/T=0 (timer), M1=0, M0=1 -> 0001 (high nibble)
        ; T0: Gate=0, C/T=1 (counter), M1=1, M0=0 -> 0110 (low nibble)
        ; TMOD = 0001 0110B = 16H
        MOV     TMOD, #16H

        ; Load Timer 0: mode 2, reload value = 0xFF
        MOV     TH0, #0FFH     ; Auto-reload value
        MOV     TL0, #0FFH     ; Initial count (first pulse causes overflow)

        ; Load Timer 1 for 50 ms (50000 counts @ 1 µs)
        ; Reload value = 65536 - 50000 = 15536 = 3CB0H
        MOV     TH1, #3CH      ; High byte = 3CH
        MOV     TL1, #0B0H     ; Low byte  = B0H

        ; Enable interrupts
        SETB    ET0            ; Enable Timer 0 interrupt
        SETB    ET1            ; Enable Timer 1 interrupt
        SETB    EA             ; Enable global interrupts

        ; Start both timers
        SETB    TR0            ; Start Timer 0 (counter)
        SETB    TR1            ; Start Timer 1 (timer)

        ; Infinite idle loop
LOOP:
        SJMP    LOOP           ; Wait for interrupts

;------------------------------------------------------------------
; TIMER0_ISR: Counter interrupt for touch detection
;------------------------------------------------------------------
TIMER0_ISR:
        ; Interrupt flag TF0 is automatically cleared by hardware
        INC     TOUCH_COUNT    ; Increment touch count
        MOV     P1, TOUCH_COUNT ; Output to Port P1
        RETI                   ; Return from interrupt

;------------------------------------------------------------------
; TIMER1_ISR: Timer interrupt for 1-second periodic update
;------------------------------------------------------------------
TIMER1_ISR:
        ; Timer 1 mode 1 requires manual reload
        MOV     TH1, #3CH      ; Reload high byte for 50 ms
        MOV     TL1, #0B0H     ; Reload low byte

        ; Decrement 1-second counter
        DJNZ    OVERFLOW_CNT, EXIT_T1  ; Jump if not zero

        ; 1 second elapsed: output touch count to Port P2
        MOV     P2, TOUCH_COUNT
        MOV     OVERFLOW_CNT, #14H     ; Reset counter (20)

EXIT_T1:
        RETI                   ; Return from interrupt

;------------------------------------------------------------------
        END                    ; End of program

---

#### **Program Explanation**

1. **Timer 0 Configuration (Counter Mode)**
   - **TMOD** setting: `0x16` (binary `00010110`).
     - Timer 0: `C/T = 1` (counter), `M1M0 = 10` (mode 2, 8-bit auto-reload).
     - Timer 1: `C/T = 0` (timer), `M1M0 = 01` (mode 1, 16-bit).
   - **TH0 = TL0 = 0xFF**: The counter starts at 255; the first pulse on P3.4 increments it to 0, causing an overflow and an interrupt. The auto-reload feature immediately resets it to 0xFF for the next pulse.
   - Each pulse generates a Timer 0 interrupt, where `touch_count` is incremented and sent to Port P1.

2. **Timer 1 Configuration (1-Second Timing)**
   - With a 12 MHz crystal, the timer increments every 1 $\mu$s.
   - To achieve a 1-second period, we use a **50 ms** overflow and count 20 overflows.
   - Reload value for 50 ms:  
     $$ \text{Counts} = 50000 = 50000_{10} = \text{C350H} $$  
     $$ \text{Reload} = 65536 - 50000 = 15536_{10} = \text{3CB0H} $$
   - In the Timer 1 ISR, we reload TH1/TL1 with `3CB0H`, decrement `OVERFLOW_CNT`, and when it reaches zero, output `touch_count` to Port P2.

3. **Interrupt Handling**
   - **Timer 0 ISR**: Updates the touch count and Port P1 in real time.
   - **Timer 1 ISR**: Manages the 1-second timing and updates Port P2 periodically.
   - Both interrupts are enabled, and the main program simply waits in an infinite loop.

4. **Memory Usage**
   - `TOUCH_COUNT` at `30H`: Stores the current touch count (0-255).
   - `OVERFLOW_CNT` at `31H`: Counts 50 ms overflows (initialized to 20).
   - Stack pointer set to `60H` to avoid conflict with these variables.

---

#### **Hardware Considerations**
- The capacitive touch sensor output should be connected to **P3.4** (T0 pin).
- Ports P1 and P2 are assumed to be connected to display units (e.g., LEDs or 7-segment displays).
- If the sensor output is noisy or bounces, additional hardware or software debouncing may be required (not implemented here as per the problem statement).

---

#### **Visual Representation of System Architecture**

> **System Block Diagram**
> 
> * **Microcontroller**: 8051 core with internal timers and ports.
> * **Sensor Connection**: 
>   - Capacitive touch sensor output pin → **P3.4** (Timer 0 external input).
> * **Output Ports**:
>   - **Port P1**: Connected to 8 LEDs (real-time touch count in binary).
>   - **Port P2**: Connected to an 8-bit display (updated every second).
> * **Timers**:
>   - **Timer 0**: Configured as a **counter** (mode 2), counting pulses from P3.4.
>   - **Timer 1**: Configured as a **timer** (mode 1), generating 50 ms interrupts.
> * **Interrupt Flow**:
>   - Timer 0 overflow → ISR updates `touch_count` and Port P1.
>   - Timer 1 overflow → ISR reloads timer, counts 20 overflows, then updates Port P2.
> * **Data Paths**:
>   - Sensor pulse → Timer 0 counter → Interrupt → Increment `touch_count` → Port P1.
>   - Timer 1 interrupts → 1-second elapsed → Read `touch_count` → Port P2.

This solution meets all specified requirements: Timer 0 detects touch events, Port P1 shows the updated count on each touch, and Timer 1 triggers a periodic display on Port P2 every second.

question.content: Develop an 8051 ALP to interface a 16×2 LCD display with a suitable diagram. Assume the string "VIT" is stored in ROM location from 200H onwards and the string "Vellore" is stored in ROM location from 300H onwards. A switch is connected to port pin $P0.4$. If the switch input is $1$, display the string "VIT" in line 1 of the LCD display; otherwise, display the string "Vellore" in line 2 of the LCD display.

answer.content:
### **Introduction**
The 8051 microcontroller interfaces with a 16×2 LCD display to show strings stored in ROM. A switch connected to $P0.4$ controls the display: if the switch input is high (logic 1), the string “VIT” is displayed on line 1; if low (logic 0), “Vellore” is displayed on line 2. The program continuously monitors the switch and updates the display accordingly.

### **Circuit Diagram Description**

> **Components:**
> - **8051 Microcontroller (e.g., AT89C51):** Provides control and data signals.
> - **16×2 LCD Module (HD44780 compatible):** Displays the strings.
> - **Switch:** Momentary push‑button connected between $P0.4$ and ground.
> - **Pull‑up Resistor:** 10 kΩ external resistor connected from $P0.4$ to Vcc (if internal pull‑up is insufficient).
> - **Power Supply:** +5 V DC for the 8051 and LCD.
>
> **Connections:**
> - **LCD Data Bus (8‑bit):** $D0$–$D7$ of LCD connected to Port 2 ($P2.0$–$P2.7$).
> - **LCD Control Pins:**
>   - $RS$ (Register Select) → $P3.0$
>   - $RW$ (Read/Write) → $P3.1$ (tied low for write‑only operation)
>   - $E$ (Enable) → $P3.2$
> - **LCD Power:**
>   - $V_{SS}$ (Ground) → GND
>   - $V_{DD}$ (+5 V) → +5 V
>   - $V_{EE}$ (Contrast) → Wiper of a 10 kΩ potentiometer between +5 V and GND.
> - **Switch Connection:**
>   - One terminal → $P0.4$
>   - Other terminal → GND
>   - External pull‑up resistor from $P0.4$ to +5 V ensures a high level when the switch is open.
>
> **Clock:** 8051 crystal oscillator (typically 12 MHz) connected across $XTAL1$ and $XTAL2$.

### **Assumptions**
- The LCD operates in 8‑bit mode, with 2 lines and 5×7 character font.
- Strings are stored in code memory starting at addresses 200 H (“VIT”) and 300 H (“Vellore”), each null‑terminated.
- The switch is debounced (or the program includes a delay to avoid flickering).
- Port 0 requires an external pull‑up resistor for proper high logic level.

### **Program Explanation**
The program comprises:
1. **Initialization:** Sets the stack pointer, initializes the LCD (function set, display on, clear, entry mode).
2. **Switch Monitoring:** Reads $P0.4$; if high, displays “VIT” on line 1; if low, displays “Vellore” on line 2.
3. **LCD Routines:** Subroutines for sending commands and data, with appropriate delays.
4. **String Display:** Reads characters from ROM until a null terminator and sends them to the LCD.
5. **Main Loop:** Continuously checks the switch and updates the display.

**Pin Definitions:**
- Port 2: LCD data port.
- $P3.0$: $RS$ (0 = command, 1 = data).
- $P3.1$: $RW$ (0 = write, 1 = read; kept at 0).
- $P3.2$: $E$ (enable pulse).
- $P0.4$: Switch input.

**LCD Commands Used:**
- `CLEAR` = `01H`
- `HOME` = `02H`
- `LINE1` = `80H` (DDRAM address for first line)
- `LINE2` = `C0H` (DDRAM address for second line)

### **Assembly Language Program**

assembly
;--------------------------------------------------------------------
; 8051 ALP to interface 16x2 LCD with switch on P0.4
; Strings: "VIT" at 200H, "Vellore" at 300H
; If P0.4 = 1, display "VIT" on line1; else "Vellore" on line2
;--------------------------------------------------------------------

; Pin definitions
RS      EQU     P3.0
RW      EQU     P3.1
E       EQU     P3.2
LCD_DATA EQU    P2
SWITCH  EQU     P0.4

; LCD command constants
CLEAR   EQU     01H
HOME    EQU     02H
LINE1   EQU     80H
LINE2   EQU     0C0H

        ORG     0000H
        LJMP    MAIN

; String storage in ROM
        ORG     200H
STRING1: DB     'V','I','T',0        ; Null-terminated "VIT"

        ORG     300H
STRING2: DB     'V','e','l','l','o','r','e',0   ; Null-terminated "Vellore"

; Main program
        ORG     100H                 ; Avoid overlap with strings
MAIN:
        MOV     SP, #60H             ; Initialize stack pointer
        ACALL   LCD_INIT             ; Initialize LCD

CHECK_SWITCH:
        JB      SWITCH, DISPLAY_VIT  ; If switch high, display VIT
        ; Otherwise display Vellore on line 2
        MOV     DPTR, #STRING2       ; Pointer to "Vellore"
        MOV     A, #LINE2            ; Set cursor to line 2
        ACALL   LCD_CMD
        ACALL   DISPLAY_STRING
        SJMP    WAIT_LOOP

DISPLAY_VIT:
        MOV     DPTR, #STRING1       ; Pointer to "VIT"
        MOV     A, #LINE1            ; Set cursor to line 1
        ACALL   LCD_CMD
        ACALL   DISPLAY_STRING

WAIT_LOOP:
        ACALL   DELAY_1S             ; Wait before checking again
        SJMP    CHECK_SWITCH         ; Continuous monitoring

;--------------------------------------------------------------------
; LCD_INIT: Initialize LCD in 8-bit, 2-line, 5x7 mode
;--------------------------------------------------------------------
LCD_INIT:
        ACALL   DELAY_20MS           ; Power-on delay
        MOV     A, #38H              ; Function set: 8-bit, 2 lines, 5x7
        ACALL   LCD_CMD
        MOV     A, #0CH              ; Display on, cursor off, blink off
        ACALL   LCD_CMD
        MOV     A, #06H              ; Entry mode: increment, no shift
        ACALL   LCD_CMD
        MOV     A, #CLEAR            ; Clear display
        ACALL   LCD_CMD
        RET

;--------------------------------------------------------------------
; LCD_CMD: Send command in accumulator to LCD
;--------------------------------------------------------------------
LCD_CMD:
        MOV     LCD_DATA, A          ; Output command to data port
        CLR     RS                   ; RS = 0 for command
        CLR     RW                   ; RW = 0 for write
        SETB    E                    ; Enable pulse high
        ACALL   DELAY_1MS            ; Pulse width
        CLR     E                    ; Enable low
        ACALL   DELAY_1MS            ; Command execution time
        RET

;--------------------------------------------------------------------
; LCD_DATA_WRITE: Send data in accumulator to LCD
;--------------------------------------------------------------------
LCD_DATA_WRITE:
        MOV     LCD_DATA, A          ; Output data to data port
        SETB    RS                   ; RS = 1 for data
        CLR     RW                   ; RW = 0 for write
        SETB    E                    ; Enable pulse high
        ACALL   DELAY_1MS            ; Pulse width
        CLR     E                    ; Enable low
        ACALL   DELAY_1MS            ; Data write time
        RET

;--------------------------------------------------------------------
; DISPLAY_STRING: Display null-terminated string pointed by DPTR
;--------------------------------------------------------------------
DISPLAY_STRING:
        PUSH    ACC                  ; Save accumulator
DS_LOOP:
        CLR     A                    ; Clear A for MOVC
        MOVC    A, @A+DPTR           ; Get character from code memory
        JZ      DS_END               ; If null, end of string
        ACALL   LCD_DATA_WRITE       ; Display character
        INC     DPTR                 ; Point to next character
        SJMP    DS_LOOP
DS_END:
        POP     ACC                  ; Restore accumulator
        RET

;--------------------------------------------------------------------
; DELAY ROUTINES (for 12 MHz crystal)
;--------------------------------------------------------------------
DELAY_1MS:                           ; Approximately 1 ms delay
        MOV     R6, #2
LOOP1:  MOV     R7, #250
        DJNZ    R7, $
        DJNZ    R6, LOOP1
        RET

DELAY_20MS:                          ; Approximately 20 ms delay
        MOV     R5, #40
LOOP2:  ACALL   DELAY_1MS
        DJNZ    R5, LOOP2
        RET

DELAY_1S:                            ; Approximately 1 second delay
        MOV     R4, #200
LOOP3:  ACALL   DELAY_20MS
        DJNZ    R4, LOOP3
        RET

;--------------------------------------------------------------------
        END

### **Flowchart**

> **Program Flow:**
> 1. **Start**
> 2. **Initialize:**
>    - Set stack pointer
>    - Initialize LCD (function set, display on, clear display)
> 3. **Check Switch:**
>    - Read $P0.4$
>    - **If $P0.4 = 1$:**
>        - Set DDRAM address to line 1 (`80H`)
>        - Display string from 200 H (“VIT”)
>    - **Else ($P0.4 = 0$):**
>        - Set DDRAM address to line 2 (`C0H`)
>        - Display string from 300 H (“Vellore”)
> 4. **Delay** (1 s)
> 5. **Loop** back to step 3.
>
> **Subroutines:**
> - `LCD_CMD`: Send command in $A$ to LCD.
> - `LCD_DATA_WRITE`: Send data in $A$ to LCD.
> - `DISPLAY_STRING`: Display null‑terminated string pointed by $DPTR$.
> - Delay routines (`DELAY_1MS`, `DELAY_20MS`, `DELAY_1S`).

### **Note on Port 0 Configuration**
Port 0 of the 8051 is open‑drain and lacks internal pull‑up resistors. Therefore, an external 10 kΩ pull‑up resistor on $P0.4$ is necessary to ensure a logic high when the switch is open. Writing a ‘1’ to the port pin configures it as an input but does not provide a strong high; the external resistor is essential.

### **Conclusion**
The provided assembly program successfully interfaces the 8051 with a 16×2 LCD and a switch. The LCD displays “VIT” on the first line when the switch is high, and “Vellore” on the second line when the switch is low. The circuit diagram and code are detailed for easy implementation.

question.content: An 8051 microcontroller is connected to a linear temperature sensor through ADC0804. The sensor output voltage is linearly proportional to temperature, with $0\text{V}$ at $0^{\circ}\text{C}$ and $5\text{V}$ at $100^{\circ}\text{C}$. At $100^{\circ}\text{C}$, the ADC0804 receives $5\text{V}$. Develop an ALP with a suitable interfacing diagram to interface ADC0804 with 8051. Read the ADC value, store it in Accumulator $A$, and compare it with the threshold ADC value corresponding to $50^{\circ}\text{C}$ (assume an 8-bit ADC with $V_{\text{ref}}=5\text{V}$, so the threshold is $128$). If $A > \text{threshold}$, turn ON the buzzer connected to port P1.3 to alarm for $60\text{ ms}$ using timer 0.

Component Connection to 8051
ADC0804 CS P2.0 (active low)
ADC0804 RD P2.1 (active low)
ADC0804 WR P2.2 (active low)
ADC0804 INTR P3.2 (INT0)
ADC0804 D0-D7 Port 0
Buzzer P1.3

answer.content:
### Interfacing Diagram

> **Visual Representation: ADC0804 Interfacing with 8051 Microcontroller**
> 
> **Components and Connections:**
> 
> 1. **8051 Microcontroller** (40-pin DIP):
>    - **Port 0 (P0.0–P0.7)**: Connected to ADC0804 data lines DB0–DB7 (pins 11–18).
>    - **Port 1 (P1.3)**: Connected to the buzzer (via a current-limiting resistor and optional transistor driver).
>    - **Port 2**:
>        - **P2.0**: Connected to WR (pin 3) of ADC0804.
>        - **P2.1**: Connected to RD (pin 2) of ADC0804.
>        - **P2.2**: Connected to INTR (pin 5) of ADC0804.
>    - **Power and Clock**:
>        - **Vcc (pin 40)**: +5 V.
>        - **GND (pin 20)**: Ground.
>        - **EA (pin 31)**: Tied high (via pull-up resistor) for internal program memory.
>        - **Crystal**: 12 MHz across pins 18 (XTAL2) and 19 (XTAL1) with two 22 pF capacitors to ground.
> 
> 2. **ADC0804** (20-pin DIP):
>    - **Pin 1 (CS)**: Connected to GND (always selected).
>    - **Pin 2 (RD)**: Connected to P2.1 of 8051.
>    - **Pin 3 (WR)**: Connected to P2.0 of 8051.
>    - **Pin 4 (CLK IN) and Pin 19 (CLK R)**: Connected together via an RC network (10 kΩ resistor to Vcc and 150 pF capacitor to GND) to generate the internal clock (~640 kHz).
>    - **Pin 5 (INTR)**: Connected to P2.2 of 8051.
>    - **Pin 6 (Vin+)**: Connected to the temperature sensor output.
>    - **Pin 7 (Vin-)**: Connected to GND.
>    - **Pin 8 (AGND) and Pin 10 (DGND)**: Connected to common ground.
>    - **Pin 9 (Vref/2)**: Connected to a 2.5 V reference (e.g., voltage divider with two 4.7 kΩ resistors from Vcc to GND, or a precision 2.5 V reference IC). This sets the full-scale input voltage to 5 V.
>    - **Pins 11–18 (DB0–DB7)**: Connected to P0.0–P0.7 of 8051.
>    - **Pin 20 (Vcc)**: Connected to +5 V.
> 
> 3. **Temperature Sensor**:
>    - Output (linear voltage: 0 V at 0°C, 5 V at 100°C) connected to ADC0804 Vin+ (pin 6).
>    - Ground connected to common GND.
>    - Supply voltage (if required) appropriately derived from +5 V.
> 
> 4. **Buzzer**:
>    - One terminal connected to P1.3 of 8051 through a 1 kΩ current-limiting resistor.
>    - The other terminal connected to GND (assuming active-high buzzer). For higher current, an NPN transistor driver may be inserted between P1.3 and the buzzer.
> 
> **Layout Description**:
> - The 8051 is placed centrally on the left, with the ADC0804 to its right.
> - The temperature sensor is placed left of the ADC0804, connected via a short wire to Vin+.
> - The buzzer is placed near the 8051, connected to Port 1.
